checks.a_analysis: The output text accurately reflects the content of the source document without inventing paragraphs or sentences. Document titles and alt text are AI-generated as permitted.
All URLs found in the source document (pages 9 and 18) were accurately preserved in the output. No external contact information was fabricated.
All sections and pages from the source document are represented in the output. Blank pages (2, 4, 17) are correctly noted as intentionally blank.

In Appendix B (Page 19), the source contains a Gantt chart displaying approximately 30 project rows across 8 quarters. The output identifies this structure and creates a table (element index 202) but fails to populate it with any data rows, including only the header row. While the project names are captured as a flat list in paragraph index 19, the essential data mapping projects to specific quarters is entirely omitted.
checks.b_evidence_source: Gantt chart on page 19 listing approximately 30 projects (e.g., 'DES Release II', 'NC DES SCUBI Integration') and their quarterly status.
checks.b_evidence_output: The table at element index 202 contains only the header row ('Categories', '2015 Q1', ..., '2016 Q4') and 0 data rows.
checks.b_evidence_location: Appendix B - GDAC Portfolio, element index 202 (Page 19)

checks.check_name: Significant text omission within a section
checks.a_analysis: Beyond the structured data failure in Appendix B (noted in C4), the body text and paragraphs within each section are complete.

checks.check_name: Minor text differences beyond acceptable reformatting
checks.a_analysis: On page 11, under the 'Medicaid Analytics Pilot' section, a word choice error occurred where 'care' was replaced with 'article', changing the meaning of the sentence slightly.
checks.b_evidence_source: ...and the cost associated with that particular care.
checks.b_evidence_output: ...and the cost associated with that particular article.
checks.b_evidence_location: Division of Medicaid Assistance - Medicaid Analytics Pilot, element index 121 (Page 11)

checks.a_analysis: I have reviewed the output against the source document. While several elements like the 'Department of Information Technology' footer appear multiple times, this is consistent with the source document where they appear on every page. No instances of accidental content duplication (paragraphs or sections repeated twice) were found.

The heading hierarchy is inconsistent and incorrectly nested compared to the source document's structure and Table of Contents. Major sections that are peers in the source (e.g., 'Criminal Justice and Public Safety', 'Child and Family Safety', 'Fraud and Compliance') are assigned different levels (H2 and H3). Additionally, sub-sections like 'P20W System' are promoted to H2, making them peers to their parent section 'Longitudinal and Performance Systems'.
checks.b_evidence_source: Table of Contents (Page 3) shows 'Criminal Justice and Public Safety', 'Child and Family Safety', and 'Fraud and Compliance' at the same indentation level.
checks.b_evidence_output: 'Criminal Justice and Public Safety' is level: 2 (element index 52). 'Child and Family Safety' is level: 3 (element index 64). 'Fraud and Compliance' is level: 3 (element index 76).

checks.a_analysis: The main data table in Section 16 correctly identifies headers and maintains the column/row relationship from the source. Although an empty table structure was generated for Appendix B (where a chart exists in the source), the structural fidelity of actual tables present in the text is preserved.

The output follows the logical flow of the source document, processing pages sequentially and maintaining the order of content blocks within pages. Section continuations across page breaks (like the 'Deceased Matching' section) are handled correctly in the reading order.

Several lists throughout the document (e.g., the requirements list in the Legislative Reporting section, bulleted activities under CJLEADS, and CPS Pilot integration points) have been flattened into plain text paragraph blocks. This results in a loss of semantic list structure, although the content remains readable.
checks.b_evidence_source: Page 5 (Report Page 3): 'a. The funding, expenditures...' through 'b. The contribution of funds...'
checks.b_evidence_output: The list items are merged into a single paragraph element (element index 47) with no list tags.

checks.a_analysis: Multiple functional URLs present in the source document are converted as plain text within paragraph blocks rather than as structured, clickable hyperlink elements. This constitutes missing links in the structural output.
checks.b_evidence_output: URLs are represented as plain text within paragraph content (element index 88 and 200).

checks.a_analysis: The HTML output faithfully represents the visual organization of the source PDF. The cover page information, table of contents, and all content sections are presented in the same logical order. The conversion from a fixed-page PDF layout to a responsive single-column HTML layout is handled correctly, preserving the hierarchical structure and visual flow of the original document. Appendix B, which is a complex visual Gantt chart in the PDF, has been converted into a table format in the HTML, which is a standard accessible alternative for such data visualizations and maintains the organization of the information.

The tables in the document, specifically the 'NC GDAC Project Contribution by SAS' table on page 16 and the data table representing the Gantt chart in Appendix B, are rendered correctly in the HTML. They maintain clear column headers and data rows, ensuring the information is readable and understandable for a sighted user. There are no signs of overlapping content, misaligned columns, or missing borders that would impede comprehension.

The meaningful images from the source PDF are present in the HTML output. The North Carolina (NC) logo and the 'Information Technology' branding from the cover page are correctly rendered. The complex Gantt chart in Appendix B has been replaced with a table, which is an intentional and appropriate accessibility improvement rather than a missing image defect.

A review of the rendered HTML confirms that there is no unintended duplication of body content. While headings appear in both the navigation/table of contents area and the main body, this is an expected feature of accessible document design. No paragraphs, tables, or sections of body content are repeated within the document flow.
